sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems
topicArea: Amino Acid Chemistry: pI, Titration & Zwitterions

questions.passage: A biochemist investigates the acid-base properties of amino acids. She titrates 50.0 mL of 0.10 M glycine (Gly, pKa₁ = 2.34 for the carboxyl group, pKa₂ = 9.60 for the amino group) with 0.10 M NaOH, monitoring pH. At physiological pH (~7.4), glycine exists predominantly as a zwitterion (⁺H₃N-CH₂-COO⁻). She also performs a titration of glutamic acid (Glu) under the same conditions. The resulting titration curve for glutamic acid is shown in Figure 1. She additionally studies lysine (Lys, pKa₁ = 2.18, pKa₂ = 8.95, pKa₃ = 10.53) to understand how ionizable side chains affect the isoelectric point.
questions.stem: The isoelectric point (pI) of glycine is calculated as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: pI = (pKa₁ + pKa₂)/2 = (2.34 + 9.60)/2 = 5.97 — the average of the two pKa values flanking the zwitterionic form

choices.label: B
choices.text: pI = pKa₁ × pKa₂ = 22.5

choices.label: C
choices.text: pI = pKa₂ − pKa₁ = 7.26

choices.label: D
choices.text: pI = pKa₁ only = 2.34

explanations.A: Correct. The isoelectric point is the pH at which the amino acid has zero net charge (exists predominantly as the zwitterion). For amino acids without ionizable side chains, pI = (pKa₁ + pKa₂)/2. For glycine: pI = (2.34 + 9.60)/2 = 5.97. At this pH, equal amounts of the cationic and anionic forms exist, and the zwitterion dominates. At pH < pI, the amino acid carries a net positive charge; at pH > pI, it carries a net negative charge. This determines electrophoretic migration direction.
explanations.B: The pI is an average (sum divided by 2), not a product. Multiplying pKa values has no chemical meaning.
explanations.C: The difference between pKa values relates to the buffering range but is not the pI. The pI is their average, not their difference.
explanations.D: pKa₁ alone represents the pH at which the fully protonated form (⁺H₃N-CH₂-COOH) is half-deprotonated to the zwitterion. It is not the pI, which requires averaging the two pKa values that flank the zwitterion.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Titration of glutamic acid with NaOH.

questions.stem: At physiological pH (7.4), glycine exists primarily as a zwitterion because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: neither the carboxyl nor amino group is ionized at pH 7.4

choices.label: B
choices.text: pH 7.4 equals exactly pKa₁

choices.label: C
choices.text: the molecule has no ionizable groups

choices.label: D
choices.text: the carboxyl group is deprotonated (COO⁻, since pH > pKa₁) and the amino group is protonated (NH₃⁺, since pH < pKa₂) — yielding a molecule with both a positive and negative charge but zero net charge

explanations.A: Both groups are ionized at pH 7.4. The carboxyl group (pKa₁ = 2.34) is fully deprotonated at pH 7.4 (since pH >> pKa₁), and the amino group (pKa₂ = 9.60) is fully protonated at pH 7.4 (since pH << pKa₂). Both charges are present simultaneously.
explanations.B: pH 7.4 is far above pKa₁ (2.34). At pKa₁, half the molecules have the carboxyl protonated and half have it deprotonated. At pH 7.4, essentially all carboxyl groups are deprotonated.
explanations.C: Glycine has two ionizable groups — the α-carboxyl and α-amino groups. The zwitterionic form exists precisely because both groups are ionized (one positive, one negative).
explanations.D: Correct. At pH 7.4: the carboxyl group (pKa₁ = 2.34) is deprotonated because pH >> pKa₁ → COO⁻. The amino group (pKa₂ = 9.60) is protonated because pH << pKa₂ → NH₃⁺. The result is the zwitterion ⁺H₃N-CH₂-COO⁻ with both charges present but zero net charge. The zwitterionic form explains amino acid properties: high melting points (ionic interactions), water solubility, and amphoteric behavior (can act as acid or base).

questions.stem: The pI of glutamic acid (pKa₁ = 2.19, pKa₂ = 4.25, pKa₃ = 9.67) is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: (2.19 + 9.67)/2 = 5.93 — average of the first and last pKa

choices.label: B
choices.text: (4.25 + 9.67)/2 = 6.96 — average of pKa₂ and pKa₃

choices.label: C
choices.text: (2.19 + 4.25)/2 = 3.22 — the average of the two pKa values flanking the zwitterionic form, which for an acidic amino acid are pKa₁ (α-COOH) and pKa₂ (side chain COOH)

choices.label: D
choices.text: 9.67 (the highest pKa only)

explanations.A: Averaging the first and last pKa works only for amino acids without ionizable side chains (like glycine). For acidic amino acids, the zwitterion form is flanked by pKa₁ and pKa₂ (not pKa₃).
explanations.B: Averaging pKa₂ and pKa₃ would give the pI of a basic amino acid (like lysine), where the zwitterion is flanked by those two values. For glutamic acid, this incorrectly skips over the side chain carboxyl.
explanations.C: Correct. For acidic amino acids (Asp, Glu), pI = (pKa₁ + pKa₂)/2, where both pKa values correspond to carboxyl groups (α-COOH and side-chain COOH). The zwitterion of Glu has NH₃⁺, COO⁻ (α), and COOH (side chain). Losing the side-chain proton (pKa₂ = 4.25) gives net negative charge. Gaining a proton on the α-carboxyl (pKa₁ = 2.19) gives net positive charge. The pI lies between them: (2.19 + 4.25)/2 = 3.22. The general rule: average the two pKa values flanking the form with zero net charge.
explanations.D: Using only the highest pKa gives the pH at which the amino group is half-deprotonated, not the pI. The pI requires averaging two pKa values.

questions.topic: pI of Acidic Amino Acids / Glutamic Acid

questions.stem: The pI of lysine (pKa₁ = 2.18, pKa₂ = 8.95, pKa₃ = 10.53) is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: (8.95 + 10.53)/2 = 9.74 — the average of the two pKa values flanking the zwitterionic form, which for a basic amino acid are pKa₂ (α-NH₃⁺) and pKa₃ (side chain NH₃⁺)

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2.18 only

explanations.A: Correct. For basic amino acids (Lys, Arg, His), pI = (pKa₂ + pKa₃)/2, where both pKa values correspond to amino groups (α-NH₃⁺ and side-chain NH₃⁺). The zwitterion of Lys has NH₃⁺ (α), COO⁻ (α), and NH₃⁺ (side chain) — net charge +1. Losing the α-amino proton (pKa₂ = 8.95) gives zero net charge. Losing the side-chain proton (pKa₃ = 10.53) gives net charge −1. The pI lies between them: (8.95 + 10.53)/2 = 9.74. This high pI means lysine carries a positive charge at physiological pH.
explanations.B: For a basic amino acid, pKa₁ and pKa₂ do not flank the zero-net-charge species. At pH 5.57, lysine would still carry a net positive charge from the protonated side-chain amino group.
explanations.C: Averaging pKa₁ and pKa₃ skips over the relevant protonation states and gives an incorrect intermediate value.
explanations.D: pKa₁ alone is the pH at which the α-carboxyl is half-deprotonated. This is far from the pI of a basic amino acid.

questions.topic: pI of Basic Amino Acids / Lysine

questions.stem: During the titration of glycine with NaOH, the solution has maximum buffering capacity at:

choices.label: A
choices.text: pH 5.97 (the pI)

choices.label: B
choices.text: pH 7.00 (neutral water)

choices.label: C
choices.text: pH 2.34 and pH 9.60 — at each pKa, the concentrations of the protonated and deprotonated forms are equal, providing maximum buffering (Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa when [A⁻] = [HA])

choices.label: D
choices.text: pH 14.00 only

explanations.A: At the pI (5.97), glycine is predominantly in the zwitterionic form with minimal concentrations of the cationic or anionic forms. This is actually a point of minimum buffering capacity — the amino acid is between its two buffering ranges.
explanations.B: pH 7.00 has no special significance for glycine buffering. Maximum buffering occurs at the pKa values, where [HA] = [A⁻] for the relevant equilibrium.
explanations.C: Correct. Maximum buffering capacity occurs at pH = pKa, where [conjugate acid] = [conjugate base] (Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]); when [A⁻] = [HA], log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa). Glycine has two buffering regions: around pKa₁ = 2.34 (carboxyl group equilibrium: COOH ⇌ COO⁻ + H⁺) and around pKa₂ = 9.60 (amino group equilibrium: NH₃⁺ ⇌ NH₂ + H⁺). Effective buffering range is typically pKa ± 1. Between the two pKa values, buffering capacity is minimal.
explanations.D: pH 14 is far above any glycine pKa. At this pH, all ionizable groups are fully deprotonated and the solution has no remaining buffering capacity from glycine.

questions.passage: A protein chemist runs SDS-PAGE on a mixture of proteins and then performs isoelectric focusing (IEF) as a second dimension. In IEF, proteins migrate through a pH gradient gel until they reach the pH equal to their pI, where they have zero net charge and stop migrating. She applies this to separate hemoglobin variants: normal HbA (pI = 6.87) and sickle HbS (pI = 7.09), which differs by a single amino acid substitution (Glu6Val in the β-chain — a negatively charged glutamic acid replaced by a nonpolar valine).
questions.stem: In IEF, HbS migrates farther toward the cathode (basic end) than HbA because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: HbS is smaller than HbA and moves faster through the gel matrix

choices.label: B
choices.text: HbS has a higher pI (7.09 vs 6.87) — the Glu→Val substitution removes a negative charge, increasing the pI, so HbS reaches zero net charge at a more basic pH position in the gradient

choices.label: C
choices.text: HbS has more disulfide bonds than HbA

choices.label: D
choices.text: HbS binds more SDS molecules than HbA

explanations.A: IEF separates by charge (pI), not by size. All proteins eventually stop migrating at their pI regardless of molecular weight. Size-based separation is the role of SDS-PAGE (the other dimension in 2D electrophoresis).
explanations.B: Correct. The Glu6Val substitution removes one negative charge per β-chain (two per hemoglobin tetramer). With less negative charge, HbS has a higher pI (more basic) than HbA. In a pH gradient with the cathode at the basic end, HbS migrates farther toward the cathode before reaching its pI and stopping. This single amino acid change (charged → nonpolar) shifts the pI by 0.22 units — enough for clear IEF separation. This principle is why 2D gel electrophoresis (IEF + SDS-PAGE) is so powerful: it separates by both charge and size.
explanations.C: Hemoglobin lacks disulfide bonds. The Glu→Val substitution changes charge, not cysteine content. Even if disulfide bonds were present, they do not determine pI in IEF (which separates by overall net charge at the isoelectric point).
explanations.D: IEF is performed without SDS (which would mask intrinsic charge). SDS-PAGE uses SDS to create uniform charge-to-mass ratios for size-based separation. In IEF, intrinsic protein charge determines migration.

questions.stem: In the SDS-PAGE dimension, HbA and HbS would be expected to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: separate clearly because they have very different molecular weights

choices.label: B
choices.text: migrate to identical positions — the Glu→Val substitution does not significantly change molecular weight, and SDS-PAGE separates solely by size after SDS masks intrinsic charge differences

choices.label: C
choices.text: not enter the gel because they are too small

choices.label: D
choices.text: separate based on their different pI values

explanations.A: The Glu→Val substitution changes one amino acid (Glu MW ~147 → Val MW ~117, a difference of ~30 Da per subunit). For hemoglobin (~64,500 Da), this is a ~0.09% difference — far below the resolution of standard SDS-PAGE.
explanations.B: Correct. SDS-PAGE separates proteins by molecular weight. SDS binds uniformly (~1.4 g SDS per g protein), masking intrinsic charge and giving all proteins a similar charge-to-mass ratio. Migration then depends on size (smaller proteins migrate faster through the polyacrylamide sieve). Since HbA and HbS differ by only ~60 Da out of ~64,500, they are indistinguishable on SDS-PAGE. This is why 2D electrophoresis is needed — IEF resolves the charge difference that SDS-PAGE cannot detect.
explanations.C: Hemoglobin (~64.5 kDa) easily enters standard SDS-PAGE gels. Very large proteins or complexes might have difficulty entering, but hemoglobin is well within the separation range.
explanations.D: SDS-PAGE does not separate by pI. SDS coating masks intrinsic charge differences. pI-based separation is the function of IEF (the first dimension).

questions.stem: At pH 1.0, glycine would carry a net charge of approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: +1 — at pH well below both pKa values, both the carboxyl group (COOH, protonated) and amino group (NH₃⁺, protonated) are in their protonated forms, giving a net charge of +1

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0 (zwitterion)

choices.label: C
choices.text: −1 (fully deprotonated)

explanations.A: Correct. At pH 1.0 (well below pKa₁ = 2.34): carboxyl is protonated (COOH, neutral) and amino is protonated (NH₃⁺, charge +1). Net charge = +1. The fully protonated form is ⁺H₃N-CH₂-COOH. In electrophoresis at pH 1.0, glycine would migrate toward the cathode (negative electrode). As pH increases past pKa₁, the carboxyl deprotonates → zwitterion (charge 0). Past pKa₂, the amino deprotonates → anion (charge −1).
explanations.B: The zwitterion (net charge 0) exists predominantly between pKa₁ and pKa₂ (approximately pH 2.34 to 9.60). At pH 1.0, which is below pKa₁, the carboxyl group is protonated (COOH), so the molecule carries a net positive charge.
explanations.C: At pH 1.0, which is well below both pKa values (2.34 and 9.60), all groups are protonated. The amino group is NH₃⁺ (+1) and the carboxyl is COOH (0). Net charge = +1, not −1.
explanations.D: +2 would require two positive charges. Glycine has only one ionizable group that can carry a positive charge (the amino group). The carboxyl group is either neutral (COOH) or negative (COO⁻), never positive.

questions.topic: Amino Acid Charge at Extreme pH

questions.stem: A protein with a pI of 5.0 is placed in an electrophoresis gel at pH 8.0. The protein would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: not move because it has no charge at any pH

choices.label: B
choices.text: migrate toward the cathode (negative electrode) because it carries a net positive charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: migrate toward the anode (positive electrode) — at pH 8.0, which is above the protein's pI of 5.0, the protein carries a net negative charge and migrates toward the positive electrode

choices.label: D
choices.text: precipitate immediately and not enter the gel

explanations.A: Proteins have zero net charge only at their pI. At any other pH, they carry a net charge and will migrate in an electric field.
explanations.B: At pH > pI, the protein has a net negative charge (not positive). Negative charges migrate toward the anode (positive electrode). At pH < pI, the protein would be positive and migrate toward the cathode.
explanations.C: Correct. When pH > pI, deprotonation of ionizable groups exceeds protonation, giving the protein a net negative charge. Negatively charged molecules migrate toward the anode (positive electrode) in an electric field. For this protein: at pH 5.0 (= pI), net charge = 0 (no migration). At pH 8.0 (3 units above pI), many acidic residues are deprotonated (COO⁻) while basic residues may retain some protons — net negative. The greater the difference between pH and pI, the greater the net charge and faster the migration.
explanations.D: Proteins are most likely to precipitate at their pI (where they have no net charge and minimal solubility due to reduced electrostatic repulsion). At pH 8.0 (above pI), the protein carries negative charge, which increases solubility through charge-charge repulsion.

questions.stem: Histidine (pKa of imidazole side chain ≈ 6.0) is frequently found in enzyme active sites because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: its side chain is permanently charged at all physiological pH values

choices.label: B
choices.text: its side chain cannot participate in any chemical reactions

choices.label: C
choices.text: it is the largest amino acid and fills the active site completely

choices.label: D
choices.text: its imidazole pKa (~6.0) is close to physiological pH, allowing it to act as both a proton donor and acceptor — making it an effective general acid-base catalyst in enzymatic reactions

explanations.A: Histidine's imidazole side chain (pKa ~6.0) is partially protonated at physiological pH (~7.4). It is not permanently charged — roughly 4% protonated at pH 7.4 (from Henderson-Hasselbalch: 7.4 = 6.0 + log([base]/[acid]) → [base]/[acid] ≈ 25). But its pKa is close enough to physiological pH that small environmental changes in the active site can shift the protonation state.
explanations.B: Histidine is one of the most catalytically versatile amino acids precisely because it can donate and accept protons near physiological pH. It participates in acid-base catalysis, nucleophilic catalysis, and metal ion coordination (His residues coordinate Zn²⁺ in carbonic anhydrase and carboxypeptidase).
explanations.C: Histidine is a medium-sized amino acid (MW 155). Tryptophan (MW 204) is the largest. Active site function depends on chemical properties, not physical size.
explanations.D: Correct. With pKa ~6.0, histidine's imidazole ring can exist in both protonated (imidazolium, acid) and deprotonated (imidazole, base) forms near physiological pH. This makes it an effective general acid-base catalyst — it can donate a proton to one substrate (acting as an acid) and accept a proton from another (acting as a base) within a single catalytic cycle. Classic examples: serine proteases (His in the catalytic triad Ser-His-Asp), RNase A (His12 and His119), and carbonic anhydrase.
